network.overall_strategy: This network centers valine synthesis on the literature-supported Strecker route from isobutyraldehyde, hydrogen cyanide, and ammonia, while explicitly modeling the major bottleneck: low-selectivity prebiotic access to the branched C4 precursor isobutyraldehyde. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced-carbon hubs such as formate, acetaldehyde, and pyruvate under moderate alkaline-vent conditions, avoiding prolonged exposure to black-smoker temperatures that destroy amino acids. Surface environments generate and concentrate HCN, formamide, aminonitriles, and hydantoin intermediates under wet-dry and UV-driven conditions with mineral mediation. A second, more speculative but metabolically congruent branch converts pyruvate-derived carbon into alpha-ketoisovalerate-like chemistry followed by mineral-catalyzed reductive amination to valine. Ten target-reaching pathways are represented by recombinations of the validated Strecker core, aminonitrile stabilization, Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin chemistry, exogenous meteoritic delivery of valine, and the weaker keto-acid alternative. Speculative steps are explicitly marked where the literature identifies unresolved gaps, especially branched aldehyde and alpha-ketoisovalerate generation.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 forms surface-bound formyl equivalents that are released as formate.
reactions.reasoning: CO2 reduction to formate on Fe-S minerals is a core vent reaction in iron-sulfur world and alkaline vent models; this is consistent with greigite/mackinawite-mediated carbon fixation under moderate hydrothermal conditions and the thermodynamic favorability of amino-acid precursor synthesis in alkaline vents (Russell et al., 1994; Amend & Shock, 1998; Martin & Russell, 2007).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type generation of acetaldehyde from reduced carbon

conditions.other: subseafloor to vent interface, anoxic, reduced gas flux

reactions.mechanism: CO2-derived reduced carbon is converted through Fischer-Tropsch-type and related carbonyl-forming chemistry to small aldehydes including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe/Ni/Fe-oxide systems are known to generate hydrocarbons, alcohols, and aldehydes from CO/CO2 and H2, with acetaldehyde as a plausible small-carbonyl product feeding Strecker chemistry; FTT chemistry is relevant mainly as a precursor source rather than direct amino-acid synthesis (McCollom et al., 1999).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from reduced carbon

conditions.other: alkaline vent gradients, moderate hydrothermal regime favored over black-smoker conditions

reactions.mechanism: Sequential CO2 reduction and C-C bond-forming steps on Fe-S/Fe-oxide surfaces generate alpha-keto acid functionality, yielding pyruvate as a protometabolic hub.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is widely treated as a plausible hydrothermal alpha-keto acid hub in protometabolic networks and connects carbon fixation to amino-acid synthesis; moderate alkaline vent conditions are favored because hotter black-smoker regimes destroy amino acids and related products (Amend & Shock, 1998; Martin & Russell, 2007; Muchowska et al., 2019; Miller & Bada, 1988).

reactions.name: Surface generation and concentration of hydrogen cyanide

conditions.other: UV-exposed shallow pools receiving atmospheric/discharge cyanide input; this node represents surface capture and photochemical maintenance rather than a single clean stoichiometric synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Atmospherically produced or discharge-generated cyanide is photochemically maintained, concentrated, and partitioned into surface waters where it becomes available for Strecker and Bucherer-Bergs chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports HCN as a central reagent in Strecker and cyanosulfidic chemistry but not a clean direct one-step conversion from CO2 and NH3 under surface conditions; therefore this reaction is intentionally interpreted as mineral-assisted surface capture/concentration of cyanide generated by atmospheric discharge or photochemical processes (Miller, 1953; Ring et al., 1972; Miyakawa et al., 2002; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface hydrolysis/photochemical conversion of cyanide pool to formamide

conditions.other: UV-exposed evaporitic settings; drying concentrates cyanide-derived amides

reactions.mechanism: HCN-containing surface waters undergo hydration and photochemical transformation to formamide, which can accumulate in drying pools and act as a reaction medium.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is a recognized cyanide-derived prebiotic solvent/intermediate, and TiO2-mediated photochemistry supports related surface transformations; formamide-rich settings are relevant because they can stabilize aminonitriles and support broader prebiotic synthesis (Saladino et al., 2006; Green et al., 2021; Green et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Low-selectivity aldol diversification to branched aldehydes including isobutyraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: sulfide minerals and clay concentration surfaces
conditions.other: wet-dry cycling; low selectivity; represents a minor branched fraction within a complex aldehyde mixture

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde undergoes condensation/diversification in mineral-rich surface settings to a complex aldehyde distribution containing trace branched C4 aldehydes, including isobutyraldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The literature explicitly identifies branched aldehyde generation as the major bottleneck and does not support a clean high-yield acetaldehyde-to-isobutyraldehyde step. This node therefore represents low-selectivity aldol diversification, consistent with NiS-catalyzed acetylene/acetaldehyde diversification studies and the conclusion that branched aldehydes arise only as minor products in complex mixtures (Preiner et al., 2023; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Canonical Strecker synthesis of valine aminonitrile from isobutyraldehyde

conditions.other: pH approximately 9 to maximize free NH3; mild temperatures; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Isobutyraldehyde first forms an imine with NH3, then HCN adds to generate the alpha-aminonitrile intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: This is the best-validated route to valine precursors: Strecker synthesis from isobutyraldehyde, NH3, and HCN proceeds through imine formation and cyanide addition at mildly basic pH, with valine aminonitrile as the key intermediate (Miller & Van Trump, 1981; Keefe et al., 1995; Devergne et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Aminonitrile protection by N-formylation in formamide-rich surface settings

conditions.other: concentrated formamide during drying episodes; protective derivatization against Strecker reversal

reactions.mechanism: Formyl transfer from formamide generates an N-formyl aminonitrile, reducing back-reaction and preserving the aminonitrile pool.
reactions.reasoning: N-formylation of aminonitriles in formamide has been shown to stabilize Strecker products against reversion, making this an important branch that improves persistence of valine aminonitrile under prebiotic conditions (Green et al., 2021; Green et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of branched aldehyde to valine hydantoin

conditions.other: carbonate/CO2 present; hydantoin-forming branch linking aldehyde, cyanide, ammonia, and carbon dioxide chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Branched carbonyl chemistry in the presence of cyanide, ammonia, and CO2 forms a hydantoin intermediate rather than a free aminonitrile, providing an alternative route to the amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin route is literature-supported as a bridge between aldehyde-based Strecker chemistry and alpha-keto-acid logic in the presence of CO2, NH3, and cyanide (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Speculative protometabolic formation of alpha-ketoisovalerate from pyruvate-rich carbonyl chemistry

conditions.other: moderate alkaline hydrothermal regime; represented as an overall network-connecting condensation/oxidation sequence rather than a single directly demonstrated reaction

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate-derived carbonyl chemistry undergoes C-C bond formation and rearrangement through acetolactate-like protometabolic sequences to access branched alpha-keto acid space, culminating in alpha-ketoisovalerate.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies alpha-ketoisovalerate as a metabolically congruent alternative precursor to valine but emphasizes that its prebiotic formation remains experimentally unresolved. This step is therefore explicitly speculative and included only because the report identifies pyruvate-condensation-based access as a plausible open route paralleling biological logic (Copley et al., 2005; Muchowska et al., 2019; 2025 PMC report referenced in the research findings).

reactions.name: Mineral-catalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoisovalerate to valine

conditions.other: alkaline to near-neutral, anoxic; moderate temperatures preferred

reactions.mechanism: The alpha-keto acid condenses with ammonia to an imine/iminium equivalent, followed by mineral-mediated reduction to the amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids on FeS/H2S, pyrite-forming systems, iron hydroxides, and metallic nickel is experimentally supported, although substrate-specific evidence for alpha-ketoisovalerate is indirect. This is therefore plausible but less directly validated than Strecker synthesis for valine itself (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Huber & Wachtershäuser, 2003; Barge et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valine aminonitrile to valine

conditions.other: irreversible nitrile hydrolysis through amide intermediate; proceeds over geologically relevant timescales and accelerates with warming

reactions.mechanism: Sequential hydration and hydrolysis of the nitrile functionality yields the alpha-amino acid, driving the Strecker sequence forward irreversibly.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids is the canonical final step of the Strecker pathway and provides the strongest direct route from the valine aminonitrile intermediate to racemic valine (Miller & Van Trump, 1981; Devergne et al., 2021; Miller, 1998).

reactions.name: Hydrolytic deprotection of N-formyl valine aminonitrile to valine

conditions.other: geothermal cycling or prolonged aqueous exposure removes formyl protection and hydrolyzes nitrile to amino acid

reactions.mechanism: The N-formyl derivative first loses formyl protection and then undergoes nitrile hydrolysis to valine under aqueous cycling conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Formylated aminonitriles are proposed as protected reservoirs that can later return to amino-acid products under hydrolytic conditions; this reaction connects the stabilization branch to the final valine pool (Green et al., 2021; Green et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valine hydantoin to valine

conditions.other: prolonged aqueous heating in concentrated pools; slower and more condition-sensitive than aminonitrile hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin intermediate release the amino acid under sustained aqueous heating.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis is the expected completion step of the Bucherer-Bergs route, providing a literature-grounded alternative branch to valine, albeit less direct and generally harsher than simple aminonitrile hydrolysis (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Wet-dry peptide coupling of valine to valylvaline

conditions.other: repeated wet-dry cycles; effectively represents self-condensation of two valine equivalents in concentrated pools on mineral interfaces

reactions.mechanism: Dehydration during wet-dry cycling promotes ester-mediated and then amide-bond-forming condensation between concentrated valine molecules.
reactions.reasoning: Wet-dry cycles are experimentally supported for prebiotic peptide formation, and inclusion of valylvaline shows downstream relevance once valine has formed; this is post-target elaboration rather than a primary route to valine itself (Forsythe et al., 2015; Campbell et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Exogenous delivery of valine from meteoritic inventory

conditions.other: meteoritic infall and release into prebiotic surface environments; source term rather than endogenous synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Carbonaceous meteorites deliver preformed racemic valine into surface and pool environments where it mixes with endogenous prebiotic products.
reactions.reasoning: Valine is securely detected as a racemic meteoritic amino acid in Murchison and other carbonaceous chondrites at ppb levels, making exogenous delivery a legitimate source term that can merge with endogenous networks, though it is not itself a chemical transformation (Kvenvolden et al., 1970; Cronin & Pizzarello, 1983; Pizzarello & Cronin, 2000).

pathways.name: Canonical hydrothermal-to-surface Strecker route
pathways.description: Reduced carbon from alkaline hydrothermal vents yields acetaldehyde, which is diversified at the surface into trace isobutyraldehyde and then converted by canonical Strecker chemistry into valine aminonitrile followed by hydrolysis to valine.

pathways.name: Protected aminonitrile Strecker route
pathways.description: The canonical Strecker intermediate is trapped as an N-formyl aminonitrile in formamide-rich drying pools, then later hydrolyzed to valine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route
pathways.description: Surface cyanide, ammonia, carbon dioxide, and branched aldehyde feed into a hydantoin intermediate that hydrolyzes to valine.

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal keto-acid reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate chemistry accesses alpha-ketoisovalerate-like branched keto acid chemistry, followed by mineral-catalyzed reductive amination to valine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate plus surface aldehyde hybrid route
pathways.description: Pyruvate formed hydrothermally converges with hydrothermally generated acetaldehyde in a speculative branched keto-acid pathway that ends with hydrothermal reductive amination.

pathways.name: HCN-formamide-assisted Strecker route
pathways.description: Surface HCN pool is partially converted to formamide, but valine still arises through the standard isobutyraldehyde Strecker branch, emphasizing that formamide aids stabilization rather than replacing cyanide chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydantoin route with prior formamide-rich surface conditioning
pathways.description: A cyanide-rich surface environment also accumulates formamide, but valine is produced through the alternative Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin branch instead of through free aminonitrile hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Minimal validated Strecker completion route from available branched aldehyde
pathways.description: Given the presence of isobutyraldehyde from any minor environmental source, the best-supported prebiotic completion to valine is Strecker aminonitrile formation followed by hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Minimal Bucherer-Bergs completion route from available branched aldehyde
pathways.description: Given the presence of isobutyraldehyde, cyanide, ammonia, and carbon dioxide, valine can also arise through hydantoin chemistry followed by hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Exogenous meteoritic delivery route
pathways.description: Racemic valine delivered by carbonaceous meteorites enters the same downstream prebiotic pools as endogenous valine, augmenting the total inventory available for further chemistry.
